narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, family background, and planned enrollment status, among other details. For you specifically, it calculated a 63.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam—high enough that the admissions team decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. The decision hinged most heavily on your performance in later undergraduate years, as your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile of your school (far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile). This low ranking was the biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, signaling to it a potential struggle with the rigorous demands of law school and the bar. Your Year 1 grades, also in the low 2nd decile (again well under the average 5th or 6th), reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting early academic challenges that carried forward. On a more positive note, your upper-middle-class family income stood out as the third key factor, actually nudging the prediction slightly toward success compared to students from lower brackets—likely because it implies access to resources that aid preparation. Your LSAT score of 28.5 (below the dataset average of 36.7) and undergraduate GPA of 2.6 (under the typical 3.21) contributed to the overall risk profile but weren't the top drivers here. As a full-time student like over 90% of applicants, that aspect was neutral and typical. In comparison to average students who pass the bar, your decile rankings in both early and later years are notably weaker, which is why the model flagged your profile as higher-risk despite strengths like your family background. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—models like this are tools to manage limited spots—but it highlights where your record diverged from successful peers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year academics through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that speak to your growth and resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive fields. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
